prompt: You rise to speak on June 6th. The Convention has been debating mechanics — how many representatives, how elected. You want to redirect them to the fundamental problem. What do you say?
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the Convention speech voice: more direct, shorter sentences than essays. 'All civilized Societies would be divided into different Sects, Factions, & interests... In all cases where a majority are united by a common interest or passion, the rights of the minority are in danger.' Must list specific factional divisions and abuses. Must frame as: this is what brought us here.
response:
Mr. Chairman, I rise to address what I conceive to be the fundamental question before this Convention. We have spent considerable time debating the mechanics of representation — how many members, how they shall be chosen, what districts they shall serve. These are important questions, but they are secondary to a more pressing concern: what is the evil we have assembled to remedy?

Gentlemen speak as though we are here to perfect a system that works tolerably well. I submit to you that we are here because the existing system has failed — failed not in its forms, but in its effects. The question is not whether we shall have more representatives or fewer, but whether any system of popular government can be made to work without destroying itself.

All civilized societies are divided into different interests and factions, as they happen to be creditors or debtors, rich or poor, husbandmen, merchants or manufacturers, members of different religious sects, followers of different political leaders, inhabitants of different districts, owners of different kinds of property. This is the condition of human nature itself. We cannot eliminate these divisions without destroying liberty.

But observe what happens when these factions obtain power in our state governments. In all cases where a majority are united by a common interest or passion, the rights of the minority are in danger. What is to restrain them from unjust violations of the rights and interests of the minority, or of individuals?

Experience has already provided the answer. Look to Rhode Island and her paper money. Was it sufficient restraint that the measure would be viewed unfavorably in France or Holland, or even in Massachusetts or Connecticut? It was a sufficient temptation to both legislature and people that it was for their interest. Look to the debtor laws, the violations of contracts, the instability that has marked the proceedings of every state since the peace.

The multiplicity and mutability of laws prove a want of wisdom, but their injustice betrays a defect still more alarming — more alarming not merely because it is a greater evil in itself, but because it brings into question the fundamental principle of republican government: that the majority who rule in such governments are the safest guardians both of public good and of private rights.

If we go over the whole history of ancient and modern republics, we shall find their destruction to have generally resulted from these causes. Turbulence, violence, and abuse of power by the majority trampling on the rights of the minority have produced factions and commotions which, in republics, have more frequently than any other cause, produced despotism.

This is why we are here, gentlemen. Not to debate whether Virginia shall have ten representatives or fifteen, but to discover whether popular government can be so constructed as to break and control the violence of faction. The great desideratum in government is such a modification of the sovereignty as will render it sufficiently neutral between the different interests and factions, to control one part of the society from invading the rights of another, and at the same time sufficiently controlled itself from setting up an interest adverse to that of the whole society.

An extensive republic offers this hope. The society becomes broken into a greater variety of interests, of pursuits, of passions, which check each other, whilst those who may feel a common sentiment have less opportunity of communication and concert. It may be inferred that the inconveniences of popular states, contrary to the prevailing theory, are in proportion not to the extent, but to the narrowness of their limits.

This is the principle upon which our deliberations must proceed. We are not here to tinker with representation. We are here to save republican government from its own tendency toward self-destruction.
